pmos

B2B Sales — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. B2B sales — business-to-business sales, where one company sells to another rather than to a consumer — is how most software, services, and tools actually reach the organizations that use them. For a product manager, sales is the team that turns the product into revenue and carries the clearest signal about why deals are won and lost. This primer maps the base layer: what makes B2B selling different from selling to consumers, why a modern purchase is made by a committee of 6–10 people rather than one buyer, how buyers actually buy (nonlinearly, mostly without you in the room), the sales pipeline and how it differs from the funnel, who does the selling (SDRs, AEs, CSMs), the qualification frameworks (BANT and MEDDIC) that decide which deals to chase, the questioning discipline of SPIN and the insight-led Challenger approach, how to choose among them, the numbers that govern the work (win rates, sales cycles, quotas), and — most important for a PM — how product partners with sales through win-loss analysis and the field feedback loop. The named camps disagree on which selling method is best, but converge on one idea: complex B2B deals are won by helping a group of people build internal consensus, not by pitching one person. Written for any PM: every term of art is defined on first use, and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the B2B sales canon (Neil Rackham's SPIN research, Dixon & Adamson's Challenger, the MEDDIC qualification framework, Gartner's buying-journey research) plus practitioner benchmarks and win-loss sources. 16 verified sources.

What B2B sales is (and how it differs from selling to consumers)

B2B (business-to-business) sales is the practice of selling a product or service from one organization to another — a software platform sold to a company's IT department, say, rather than an app sold to a single person (which would be B2C, business-to-consumer). The defining feature is not the product but the buyer: in B2B, the "customer" is an organization, the money is usually large, and several people have to agree before anything is bought.

That scale changes everything about how the sale works. The clearest articulation of why comes from Neil Rackham's research, summarized by ReadinGraphics: small sales and large sales are fundamentally different animals. A small sale is a low-stakes, one-conversation transaction; a major sale is "high-value and complex," and "The process involves longer sales cycles (sometimes months or years), higher stakes, and requires building ongoing relationships with buyers," per the summary of Rackham's work. The tactics that close a small sale can actively hurt a large one.

To navigate that complexity, sales teams adopt a sales methodology — defined by HubSpot as "a structured framework that guides how your team approaches each stage of the sales process from initial prospecting to closing deals." Much of this primer is a tour of those frameworks. But before the frameworks, a PM needs the two things that make B2B genuinely hard: the buyers come as a group, and most of their buying happens without a salesperson present.

The buying committee: you are selling to a group, not a person

The single most important fact in modern B2B sales is that purchases are made by committees. According to Gartner research summarized by Growth Method, "the average B2B buying group involves 6–10 decision-makers, each bringing their own information and agenda." Each person sees a different slice of the problem, and each has to be convinced — which means a deal can stall because one unconvinced stakeholder you never met says no.

Those people play distinct roles. Traction Complete's map of the committee names ten, but a few matter most. The Champion is your internal advocate — "They believe in your solution, rally support, and connect you to other decision-makers." The economic or financial approver "controls the budget and judges whether the purchase is financially sound." The technical buyer "Assesses whether your solution fits within the company's technical landscape," and the business users are "The people who live with your product day to day."

The role a PM should fear most is the one that surfaces late: the Final Authority, who "Holds quiet authority to stop the deal outright, often surfacing late in the process." The practical lesson is that selling to a committee is really the work of helping a group reach internal agreement — the diagram below shows how a single deal radiates out to many people who each must say yes.

How buyers actually buy: nonlinear, and mostly without you

A second hard truth follows from the first: buyers do most of their buying on their own. Gartner's research, again via Growth Method, found that "B2B buyers spend only 17% of their total purchase journey meeting with potential suppliers. When comparing multiple suppliers, they spend just 5–6% of their time with any one sales rep." Most of the decision is formed through independent research, before and between the few conversations a salesperson gets.

The journey is also not a straight line. Rather than marching through neat stages, a buying group works through a set of "jobs." Gartner names six — "Problem Identification," "Solution Exploration," "Requirements Building," "Supplier Selection," "Validation," and "Consensus Creation" — and a group tackles them messily: "A single buying group will typically complete 6 distinct buying jobs simultaneously, looping back as new information surfaces."

For a PM, two implications matter. First, your website, documentation, and free trials are doing real selling during the 83% of the journey when no rep is present — product is part of the sales motion, not separate from it. Second, because consensus creation is itself one of the hardest jobs, the materials that help a champion sell internally (a clear one-pager, a business case, a comparison) can matter as much as a feature. The pipeline that follows is the seller's attempt to impose order on this messy reality.

The sales pipeline: the stages a deal moves through

A sales pipeline is the seller's map of every live deal. monday.com defines it as a view that "shows you exactly where every deal stands right now" and "tracks each deal from first contact to closed revenue." Deals advance through named stages, and the pipeline is the central object sales leaders use to forecast revenue and spot where deals get stuck.

The stages are fairly standard. monday.com lists seven: Prospecting — "Identify and engage companies that fit your ideal customer profile" (the ICP, or ideal customer profile, is the kind of company most likely to buy and succeed); Lead Qualification, which checks "whether prospects have the budget, authority, need, and timeline to buy"; Discovery and Demo; Proposal — laying out "what you're delivering, what it costs, and how long it takes"; Negotiation over "pricing, terms"; Closing — "signatures, payment"; and Onboarding and Expansion.

These deals live in a CRM (customer relationship management) system — the software of record for every contact and deal — which is why pipeline stages double as the shared vocabulary between sales, marketing, and product. When a PM hears "the deal slipped to next quarter" or "we lost it in procurement," they are hearing pipeline language. The diagram below shows the typical narrowing flow from many prospects to a few closed deals.

Pipeline vs funnel: two views of the same deal

"Pipeline" and "funnel" are often used loosely, but they name two different perspectives, and keeping them straight prevents a lot of cross-team confusion. Default puts the distinction in one line: "The pipeline maps what sellers do. The funnel maps what buyers do."

The funnel is the buyer's side. It "captures the buyer's journey from first touch through decision and beyond" — the familiar awareness, consideration, decision arc. The pipeline is the seller's side: "A sales pipeline tracks the seller's actions and milestones." One describes how a customer's intent matures; the other describes what the sales team does to move a deal.

The reason a PM should care is that the two views answer different questions. As Default frames it, "The funnel shows the buyer's journey, while the pipeline shows whether sales can actually convert it." Marketing usually owns the top of the funnel (generating interest); sales owns the pipeline (converting it). When a PM asks "where are we losing people?" the answer lives in the funnel; when they ask "can we close what we have?" the answer lives in the pipeline.

Who does the selling: SDRs, AEs, and CSMs

In most B2B software companies the selling job is split across three specialized roles, because no one person is good at all of prospecting, closing, and nurturing. Fullcast describes the division cleanly. The SDR (sales development representative) "builds qualified pipeline. Their primary focus is top-of-funnel activities, qualifying inbound leads, and creating new outbound opportunities so Account Executives can close."

The AE (account executive) is the closer: the AE "owns new business revenue. This role converts qualified pipeline from SDRs and marketing into closed-won deals and booked revenue." After the sale, the CSM (customer success manager) takes over to "protect and grow revenue after the sale. They ensure customers realize value, renew their contracts, and expand their investment over time." The handoff from AE to CSM is exactly where a product manager's onboarding and adoption work meets the sales motion.

Each role carries its own numbers (the benchmarks below are for SaaS, software-as-a-service — software sold as a subscription). Per Growth Spree's 2026 benchmarks, "A B2B SaaS SDR should book 8–15 meetings per month (median 11, top quartile 15+, bottom quartile under 7)," and the channel mix shifts that sharply: "Inbound-heavy SDRs (70%+ inbound) book 14–18 meetings/month. Outbound-heavy SDRs (70%+ outbound) book 6–10 meetings/month." Knowing these ratios helps a PM read why sales asks for the things it asks for — better leads, better demos, better self-serve.

Qualifying the deal: BANT and MEDDIC

Because every deal costs the team time, the first discipline of B2B sales is qualification — deciding which deals are worth pursuing. The oldest shorthand is BANT, which monday.com folds into its qualification stage as checking "whether prospects have the budget, authority, need, and timeline to buy." BANT (budget, authority, need, timeline) is a quick gut-check, useful but blunt for complex deals.

The deeper framework for complex sales is MEDDIC. Per DealHub, it "was first introduced in 1996 by a group of sales executives at PTC, a software company," and the acronym stands for "Metrics, Economic Buyer, Decision Criteria, Decision Process, Identify Pain, and Champion." Each letter is a question a rep must answer about the deal: what measurable outcome the customer wants, who controls the money, how they will decide, what steps the decision follows, what pain drives it, and who inside will advocate for it.

The element MEDDIC is most known for is the Champion — the internal advocate who, per DealHub, "feels the pain you've identified most" and pushes the deal forward from inside. The framework's value is that it forces honesty: a deal missing an economic buyer or a champion is not really qualified, no matter how enthusiastic the demo went. As DealHub puts it, "MEDDIC places an equal emphasis on assessing and qualifying potential customers."

Asking better questions: SPIN

If qualification decides which deals to chase, SPIN shapes how reps talk to buyers once they are in the room. SPIN comes from the most cited research in the field. Per ReadinGraphics, it was "developed in the 1980s from the largest-ever research study on sales performance" — "Neil Rackham and his team analyzed over 35,000 sales calls involving 10,000+ salespeople across 20+ countries over 12 years."

Highspot lays out the four question types, which give SPIN its name and are asked roughly in order. Situation questions "gauge professional needs"; problem questions uncover "the lead's specific pain points"; implication questions "explore the consequences of not investing in an offering like yours"; and need-payoff questions "encourage a small step forward" by letting the buyer name the value of solving the problem themselves.

The big finding behind SPIN is what NOT to do: Rackham's research showed that "traditional sales techniques like aggressive closing and objection handling often fail in large, complex sales." The lesson for any PM sitting in on a sales call is that the best reps talk less and ask more — they let the buyer articulate the pain, because a problem the buyer names themselves is far more motivating than one the seller asserts.

The Challenger approach: lead with insight

The most influential modern counterpoint to question-led selling is the Challenger approach, from Matt Dixon and Brent Adamson. It grew from research into five seller profiles — Problem Solver, Lone Wolf, Hard Worker, Relationship Builder, and the Challenger — and produced a counterintuitive result. Per Challenger Inc., "nearly 40% of top performers fell into the Challenger category, while Relationship Builders generated only 7% of star performers in complex sales."

The Challenger model is built on three skills: "Teach, Tailor, and Take Control." The rep teaches the customer something new about their own business, tailors that message to each stakeholder, and confidently steers the conversation — including the hard parts about price and commitment. The distinctive move is leading with a commercial insight that reframes the customer's situation, rather than opening with discovery questions.

This is where SPIN and Challenger appear to clash, and the tension is real rather than cosmetic. SPIN says draw the problem out of the buyer with questions; Challenger says bring the buyer a provocative insight they did not have. Both are evidence-based and both have large followings, which is exactly why the next section treats methodology choice as a genuine, unsettled decision rather than a solved one.

Choosing a methodology: there is no single right answer

It is tempting to ask which methodology is "best," but practitioners broadly agree there is no universal winner — this is genuinely contested terrain. WeFlow states it plainly: "There isn't one universal winner because the right framework depends on deal complexity, stakeholder count, and where your team struggles." The frameworks are tools for different jobs, not rival religions.

WeFlow's guidance is diagnostic: match the tool to the bottleneck. "MEDDIC is strong when qualification discipline is weak," "Challenger works well in complex consensus deals," and "Use SPIN for front-line questioning." They are also not mutually exclusive — a team can qualify with MEDDIC, run discovery with SPIN questions, and teach with a Challenger insight, all in the same deal.

The advice WeFlow gives is the advice a PM should internalize too: "Start by auditing your sales cycle instead of copying what another team uses." Look at where deals actually stall — early discovery, qualification, budget alignment, committee consensus, or late-stage approval — and choose the framework that fixes that specific leak. The choice is contingent on the team and the deals, not a matter of one method being correct.

The numbers: win rates, sales cycles, and quotas

B2B sales is heavily measured, and a PM who knows the rough benchmarks can read the health of a revenue engine quickly. The headline numbers are sobering. Per The Digital Bloom's 2025 benchmarks, "Overall B2B SaaS industry average win rates range from 20-30%, with the median settling at approximately 21%" — meaning roughly four of every five qualified deals are lost. Growth Spree's separate benchmark agrees closely, reporting "AE win rate median 24%."

Deals also take time. The Digital Bloom reports that "Overall B2B SaaS average sales cycle length is 84 days," with "the optimal 46–75-day window balances deal value with velocity." Conversion compounds down the funnel, too — for example "MQL-to-SQL conversion averages 12-21% across B2B sectors, with a median of approximately 15%." (An MQL, marketing-qualified lead, is a lead marketing deems promising; an SQL, sales-qualified lead, is one sales has accepted as worth working.)

The people doing the work are measured against a quota — a revenue target — usually expressed against ACV (annual contract value), the yearly revenue of a deal. Jiminny's benchmarks report "The median ACV quota is $740,000 per AE," that "On average, 66% of reps in a given group achieve quota," and — a fact PMs underrate — that "Average ramp times sits at 5.3 months," the time before a new rep is fully productive. Slow ramp and 32% attrition mean sales capacity is a constant constraint on growth.

How product partners with sales

For a product manager, the most valuable thing about sales is not the revenue — it is the signal. Sales is in the room when buyers explain why they will or will not pay, and that information is gold for the roadmap. The structured way to capture it is win-loss analysis. Clozd defines a program as "structured research with recent won and lost buyers to uncover the real decision drivers—so you can tune positioning, pricing, enablement, and roadmap."

The reason to do it formally is that internal stories about why deals are lost are usually wrong. Clozd's point is that "only direct post-decision interviews reveal why buyers ultimately chose you or your competitor," and the output should feed product directly: "Feed buyer-validated gaps and willingness-to-pay cues directly to product and pricing." Done well, loss interviews become roadmap input grounded in real buyer quotes, not hallway speculation.

Two cautions from Eficode keep the loop healthy. First, treat sales feedback as patterns, not commands: beware building "features in your product that cater to a single customer rather than meeting the needs of other customers." A loud loss does not equal a broad need. Second, get an objective reading — Eficode recommends the loss interview be run by "someone who isn't directly measured by sales targets", since a rep has every incentive to blame price or a missing feature rather than their own execution. The PM's job is to turn the noisy field signal into a small number of true, broadly-felt patterns.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

B2B sales sits at the seam between product, marketing, and revenue, and it touches several disciplines a product manager will eventually need. The topics below are the natural next steps once the fundamentals here are in place. None are taught in this primer; they are pointers to where the threads lead.

  • Go-to-market strategy — the larger plan that sales executes within: market sizing, the ideal customer profile, pricing and packaging, and the choice between sales-led and product-led motions.
  • Product-led growth — the motion where the product itself acquires and converts users with little or no sales involvement, the modern complement (and sometimes alternative) to the sales pipeline described here.
  • Sales enablement and product marketing — the function that arms reps with positioning, battlecards, demos, and the one-pagers that help a champion sell internally.
  • Pricing and packaging — how deals are structured, discounted, and tiered, which shapes negotiation and the willingness-to-pay signals win-loss analysis surfaces.
  • SaaS metrics and unit economics — the revenue picture sales feeds: annual recurring revenue, churn, net revenue retention, and the cost of acquiring a customer.
  • Customer discovery and interviewing — the research craft behind good win-loss interviews and the discovery questions that mirror SPIN's logic.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_b2b-sales.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
